# Transcrição: 08-Live Events_ Executing

- **Fonte:** 08-Live Events_ Executing
- **Duração:** 06:44
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Welcome back. Now that you have the foundation, details, and logistics for your event, how do you ensure your attendees get there and have the best experience day of? That's what I can't wait to share with you in this training. Effective communication is crucial to ensuring your attendees are informed, engaged, and excited about your event. It's better to over-communicate with your attendees than assume that they have everything they need. Here is a three-part strategy we like to use when communicating the event details with attendees. The first step is the invitation. As soon as you have all of the finalized details of your event, it's time to start sending out invitations. You'll want to include essential information such as dates, location, travel logistics, preparation guidelines, and any items they should bring. It's also great to send recommendations for hotels in the area, local things to do outside of the mastermind, and airport information. This is also a great time to get them excited about the event. A great way to generate engagement is to include a pre-event survey that can help gauge where your attendees are at and what they're hoping to get out of the event. The second step is the connection. Now, depending on when your event is, it may be weeks or even months in between the initial invitation and the actual event, but you don't want to leave your attendees in the dark in the meantime. It's important to maintain a consistent connection and keep them engaged by providing things like teasers and sneak peeks of what they can expect at the event. You can even include details about speakers, topics, and event highlights. And the third step is the check-in. Now, this is an email or a call with your attendees that will happen about two weeks before the event. Now, this is a great opportunity to confirm their attendance and verify that they are ready to go for the event. I personally prefer calling attendees as it's a more personal way to connect with them before the event, and it also allows you to check in with them and address any specific question, needs, or concerns that they may have. Now that your attendees have arrived, what happens next? It's time to bring your event to life and ensure that everything runs smoothly. You've put the work in, now let's make it happen. We're going to go through some key strategies to help you navigate the execution phase with confidence. The first strategy is pre-event alignment. Start the day of your event by meeting with your team before everyone gets there, whether it's a whole team, your friend or family who's helping you, or even if it's just yourself. And align on the schedule and the goals for the day. This will really help you feel more organized and prepared before the event kicks in the gear. You'll also want to use this time to check the event space and confirm that everything is set up correctly and looks perfect before opening the doors. From refreshments to seating arrangements, printed materials, and technology. Consider creating a checklist that night before to make sure all of the essentials are in place before the event begins. The second strategy

## [03:00 - 06:03]

is engaging with your attendees all day. This is crucial for creating a welcoming and interactive environment. Check-in is a great moment to start that initial engagement before the event begins. Have someone, whether it's you or a team member, greet them when they arrive, assist them with signing the waiver if that's something you're doing, and get their name badge. If you have someone else to guide them to their seats and show them around the room, that's even better. It's also a great opportunity to set the tone for the day and just get them excited about the event. I would also encourage you to create ongoing interaction with your attendees throughout the day. If someone brings up anything negative, address those issues promptly, and it usually turns their experience around completely. I remember an event a few years ago where a woman was at our event and she just seemed upset. She didn't seem like she was engaged with the content. She didn't seem like she was enjoying herself. And instead of steering clear and saying, I don't want to deal with that, I came up to her and I said, hey, is there anything that I can do to make your event better? And you know what she needed? A new pen. Every single pen she would pick up was running out of ink and she couldn't take notes. And I simply got her a new pen and she was so happy the rest of the event. So just keep checking with your attendees throughout the day. I would also consider sending out a post event survey. This can really help gather feedback on the event experience from your attendees and provide you areas you can improve on in the future. The third and most important strategy is embrace the unexpected. Anticipate that unforeseen issues, glitches, or scheduled deviations are going to happen during your event. And that's okay. Allow room for spontaneity and adjustments based on the energy and dynamics of the room. Sometimes speakers may go over or your attendees may want more time to network and take breaks. And that's completely okay. Be open to accommodating these requests in real time as they can really add value to the room. I can't tell you how many times I've removed entire sections from the event because Dean and Tony were over delivering. I just saved it for the next event and the attendees never noticed and were so happy. I know it's easier said than done, but prepare your mindset to embrace these moments rather than viewing them as challenges, view them as opportunities to improve your skills. The best way to do this is focus on what you can control. You won't be able to control everything that happens during your event, but you can control how you respond to those challenges. Stay flexible and adaptable. That's the fun and excitement of live events, but mainly focus on your why. Remember your event's purpose and its goal. Use that as a guiding light to navigate these unexpected situations with clarity and purpose. When you detach yourself from the equation and make it all about your attendees, it makes the hard parts worth it. And most importantly, stay positive. Your attendees will feed off of your energy. They can see you. This is live. There's no hiding behind a computer screen. When you're positive and optimistic, it inspires confidence in the event and creates an enjoyable experience for everyone. Congratulations. With the strategies

## [06:03 - 06:44]

and tactics provided in these trainings, coupled with your passion and dedication, you are now well equipped to create successful and impactful in-person events. I cannot wait to see the events that you bring to life. Remember to embrace the journey ahead with enthusiasm and a sense of joy. In-person masterminds are so much more than logistics and planning, but also an incredible opportunity to create a memorable experience, connect with your audience on a deeper level, and share knowledge that can truly inspire and transform people's lives. These training sessions will be here for you whenever you need them, and our entire team is here to provide guidance, assistance, and encouragement as you embark on this event planning journey. Good luck, and remember, you've got this.
